audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel / Shemuel Aleph chapter 25, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Samuel's burial, Carmel shearing economy, protection/payment claim, Nabal's class contempt, David's planned collective killing, Abigail's provision and intervention, bloodguilt language, Nabal's collapse, immediate marriage proposal, plural marriage, and Michal's transfer.

verses.restored: Samuel died, and all Israel gathered and mourned for him, and they buried him at his house in Ramah. David arose and went down to wilderness of Paran.
There was a man in Maon whose work was in Carmel. The man was very great: he had three thousand sheep and one thousand goats, and he was shearing his sheep in Carmel.
Name of the man was Nabal, and name of his wife was Abigail. The woman was of good understanding and beautiful in form, but the man was harsh and evil in his deeds; he was a Calebite.
David heard in wilderness that Nabal was shearing his sheep.
David sent ten young men. David said to young men, Go up to Carmel, come to Nabal, and ask him in my name concerning peace.
Thus you shall say: To life! Peace to you, peace to your house, and peace to all that is yours.
Now I have heard that you have shearers. Your shepherds were with us; we did not shame/harm them, and nothing of theirs was missing all days they were in Carmel.

Ask your young men, and they will tell you. Let my young men find favor in your eyes, because we have come on a good/festive day. Please give whatever your hand finds to your servants and to David your son.
David's young men came and spoke to Nabal according to all these words in David's name, and they rested/waited.
Nabal answered David's servants and said, Who is David, and who is son of Jesse? Today many servants are breaking away, each from before his master.
Shall I take my bread, my water, and my slaughtered meat that I slaughtered for my shearers, and give it to men whom I do not know—from where they are?
David's young men turned to their way, returned, and came and told him according to all these words.
David said to his men, Each man gird on his sword. Each man girded on his sword, and David also girded on his sword. About four hundred men went up after David, and two hundred stayed with baggage.
One young man from the young men told Abigail, Nabal's wife, saying, Look, David sent messengers from wilderness to bless/greet our master, and he screamed insults at them.
Yet the men were very good to us. We were not shamed/harmed, and we missed nothing all days we walked with them while we were in field.
They were a wall around us both night and day, all days we were with them shepherding flock.
Now know and see what you should do, because evil is determined against our master and against all his house. He is such a worthless man that no one can speak to him.

Abigail hurried and took two hundred loaves, two skins of wine, five sheep prepared, five seahs of roasted grain, one hundred clusters/cakes of raisins, and two hundred cakes of figs, and placed them upon donkeys.
She said to her young men, Pass before me; look, I am coming after you. But she did not tell Nabal her husband.
As she was riding upon donkey and descending in concealment of mountain, look, David and his men were descending toward her, and she met them.
David had said, Surely for emptiness I guarded all that belongs to this man in wilderness, so that nothing was missing from all that belonged to him; yet he returned to me evil in place of good.
Thus may Divine do to enemies of David, and thus add, if I leave from all that belongs to him until morning even one male—literally, one who urinates against wall.
Abigail saw David, hurried and descended from donkey, fell before David upon her face, and bowed to ground.
She fell at his feet and said, On me, my lord, let this guilt be on me. Please let your maidservant speak in your ears, and hear words of your maidservant.

Please do not let my lord set his heart toward this worthless man, toward Nabal, because as his name, so is he: Nabal/Folly is his name, and folly is with him. But I, your maidservant, did not see young men of my lord whom you sent.

Now, my lord, as YHWH lives and as your life lives, YHWH has restrained you from coming into bloodguilt and from saving yourself by your own hand. Now let your enemies and those seeking evil against my lord become like Nabal.
Now let this blessing/gift that your maidservant has brought to my lord be given to young men who walk in steps of my lord.

Please lift/forgive transgression of your maidservant, because YHWH will surely make for my lord an enduring house, because battles of YHWH my lord fights, and evil will not be found in you all your days.
Though a human rises to pursue you and seek your life, life of my lord shall be bound in bundle of living with YHWH your Divine, but life of your enemies YHWH shall sling from hollow of sling.
When YHWH does for my lord according to all good that YHWH has spoken concerning you and appoints you as leader over Israel,

this will not be for you a staggering or stumbling of heart to my lord: to have poured out blood without cause and for my lord to have saved himself by his own hand. When YHWH does good to my lord, remember your maidservant.
David said to Abigail, Blessed be YHWH, Divine of Israel, who sent you this day to meet me.
Blessed be your discernment, and blessed be you, who restrained me this day from coming into bloodguilt and from saving myself by my own hand.

Yet truly, as YHWH, Divine of Israel, lives, who restrained me from doing evil to you: unless you had hurried and come to meet me, surely there would not have remained to Nabal until light of morning even one male.
David received from her hand what she had brought him, and he said to her, Go up in peace to your house. See, I have heard your voice and lifted/accepted your face.

Abigail came to Nabal, and look, he had a feast in his house like feast of king. Nabal's heart was good within him, and he was very drunk, so she did not tell him a word, small or great, until light of morning.
In morning, when wine had gone out from Nabal, his wife told him these things. His heart died within him, and he became like stone.
About ten days later, YHWH struck Nabal, and he died.

David heard that Nabal was dead and said, Blessed be YHWH, who pleaded case of my reproach from hand of Nabal and restrained his servant from evil. YHWH returned evil of Nabal upon his own head. David sent and spoke concerning taking Abigail for himself as wife.
Servants of David came to Abigail at Carmel and spoke to her, saying, David sent us to you to take you for himself as wife.
She arose, bowed with face to ground, and said, Look, your maidservant is a servant to wash feet of servants of my lord.
Abigail hurried, arose, rode upon donkey, and her five young women who walked at her feet followed her. She went after messengers of David and became his wife.
David had also taken Ahinoam of Jezreel, and both of them became his wives.
Saul had given Michal his daughter, wife of David, to Palti son of Laish, who was from Gallim.

translation_notes: Abigail is the chapter's principal moral agent: she gathers intelligence, distributes resources, confronts an armed leader, names bloodguilt, and stops a household massacre.
David's protection of Nabal's shepherds may create a real social claim to generosity, but refusal of payment does not justify extortion, retaliation, or collective punishment.
The idiom 'one who urinates against wall' is a contemptuous male-targeting expression. It should not be sanitized into a heroic oath or used as gender policing.
Abigail's statement 'on me be the guilt' is strategic intercession, not confession that she caused Nabal's insult or David's planned violence.
David explicitly admits that he intended to kill every male in the household. His later kingship does not excuse the plan.
Nabal's bodily collapse and death may not be used to claim that illness, stroke, cardiac crisis, or sudden death proves Divine punishment.

David's marriage proposal arrives immediately after Nabal's death through his servants and amid a large power imbalance. Abigail's gestures and servant language do not establish modern standards of free consent.
Michal's transfer to Palti exposes royal control over women's relationships. Neither Saul nor David owns her, and she may not be treated as a dynastic object.
safety_note: Maximum safeguards: Do not romanticize David's attempted extortion or planned household massacre. Do not blame Abigail for male violence, preach strategic submission as every survivor's duty, or treat her marriage as proof that intervention earns romantic reward. Do not moralize Nabal's medical collapse, shame plural-family members, or treat Michal and Abigail as transferable property.
